You guys were awesome last year in both your giving
and
your genuine desire to give, Paul says. I think you should stay that good course and finish what you started (2 Cor. 8:10-11).
You’ll be joining good company. Poor Macedonian Jesus followers have already given generously with great love. You can too. Even more, look to Jesus, the best example
ever.
Christ made himself poor to make his followers rich. Follow his way of love and generosity for others.
Paul sees generosity as a sign of God’s Kingdom taking root. When a reconciled community works together, love overflows, burdens get shared, and God’s grace moves through people in tangible ways that bless and bring life to all.
Reflection Questions
As you read today’s passage, pay attention to the personal language Paul uses with the Corinthians. What might this suggest about the depth of relationship in Paul’s churches? How does this inform the depth of relationships we might hope for in church life today?
Paul quotes from Exodus 16:18 when describing how churches might meet each other’s needs. What does that passage reveal about how God provides for his people? How might this help you understand the believer’s role in mediating God’s provision?

Who is more honored in society: the rich or the poor? We know the answer. Those without resources are looked down on, becoming isolated and exposed to even greater suffering and loss. It's not hard to see why we end up believing survival depends on money rather than God.
Keeping and protecting wealth feels safe. Giving it away feels risky, even dangerously unwise. In this kind of culture, which is not too different from our own, Paul is attempting to teach Jesus’ way of freedom through generosity.
Paul has been reminding everyone about the ongoing famine in Jerusalem, encouraging the Corinthians to share generously and assuring them of his confidence that they’ll do the right thing (2 Cor. 9:4).
Notice Paul envisioning a world where “God is able to make
all grace
[or
every gift]
overflow to you so that, because you have enough of everything in every way at all times, you will overflow in every good work” (2 Cor. 9:8).
Having enough of everything all the time. Doesn’t that sound like Eden?
Paul knows that 1st-century Corinth is not Eden. However, he’s teaching these Jesus followers to live like it is. Since the beginning, God created humans to trust him for life. So with joy and faith, finish sending that generous gift to Jerusalem, he says.
Trust God more than your bank accounts because God’s creation is always abundant (2 Cor. 9:10-15). Watch today’s video to dive deeper into themes of generosity and abundance in the Bible.
In chapter 10, Paul pivots to a final, personal challenge for the Corinthians. Stop worrying about outward appearance. People accuse Paul of having a mighty pen but a “meh” microphone, which is no problem to him. He’s not about performance or platform-building to increase his personal fan base. He’s about loving people in Jesus’ truth and abundance.
So stop measuring success and glory with mainstream values, he says throughout the letter. Drop the obsession with celebrity and social honor by embracing sincere love for the generous and good work of Jesus instead, the kind causing people to boast not in themselves but in the Lord (2 Cor. 10:17).
Reflection Questions
Paul was convinced of God’s abundant gift, or grace, which turns death into life and exposes the falsehood of scarcity to reveal abundance throughout creation. He challenged the Corinthians to live lives transformed by this truth. Can you think of any stories from the Hebrew Scriptures that might encourage the Corinthians to trust in God’s generous provision?
How might Jesus’ willingness to generously provide life by suffering death on a cross shape the way a Christian thinks, feels, and acts toward others? How might the cross inform the human characteristics that Jesus followers most value?

Paul wraps up his letter in today’s reading, but before the final greetings, he wants a word with the so-called “super-apostles.”
Proud preachers have infiltrated the church, offering necessary respect for Paul but also declaring Paul’s inadequacy in comparison to their greater, better knowledge and teaching. Paul was cool, sure, but we’re bringing something even more. Take a look at our brilliant spiritual gifts and mystical experiences!
This “super” group might razzle and even dazzle, Paul warns, but don’t let their strutting about fool you. They look like workers of light, but it’s a disguise. And who else disguises himself as a worker of light? Yeah, the Satan.
Yikes
.
In Christ, Paul preaches the truth. But like the serpent in Eden, they deceive.
To further expose reality, Paul compares their boasting about things that make them look superior with his own boasting about suffering and weakness. I got beaten up here, kicked out of town there, and shipwrecked on an island. I even escaped mob hatred by way of a basket once.
Those super-apostles brag about hollow things the world values, and they always demand more money. If I brag about anything, it’s the way of Christ and whatever suffering is necessary to join him in it.
Paul freely suffers for the Gospel, paying his own way and unchained to social status or fear. Lives like this reveal the humble, powerful beauty of Christ and his Kingdom.
Finishing up, Paul expresses a strong desire to return to Corinth. He’s hoping for a sweet family reunion where everyone is good with one another and with God.
For everyone in 1st-century Corinth and beyond, reconciliation is always hard. It is also—
always
—the most beautiful and worthwhile pursuit.
Reflection Questions
Paul would rather boast in his weaknesses than brag about respectable accomplishments. Why do you suppose he does this? How does this mindset match his message about the paradox of the cross?
Between Paul’s two letters to the Corinthians, we get a glimpse of a church struggling to walk faithfully with Christ. Where have you seen Paul’s letters emphasize the role of the Gospel in transforming the Corinthians? What implications might this have for Christians today?

When Paul and Barnabas first traveled the Mediterranean to help Jesus’ Gospel take root, mobs sometimes brutally stoned them and left them for dead (see Acts 13-14). Still, their love for Jesus and others compelled Paul and Barnabas to start new church communities in Galatia, a Roman province.
Several years later, disagreement embroils the Galatian churches in conflict. Many begin to trust a different gospel, one that newcomers claim is superior to what Paul taught. They now require allegiance not only to Jesus but also to Jewish law and custom.
So Paul writes deeply personal expressions of shock. He’s angry in a loving way, knowing this twisted “gospel” offers no more than well-disguised enslavement. Along with his fiery pleas, Paul also saturates his letter to the Galatians with strong hope for their return to freedom, for which they had been set
free
in the first place. (Paul goes big on freedom; see Gal. 5:1.)
Paul starts the letter: Remember, the Gospel I preached came straight from God. No mediator, no books, no word of mouth. Why exchange the good news of freedom that God sent directly for old (bad) news crafted by humans with harmful agendas?
Paul sums up the good news in this letter. He tells them Jesus makes us right with God, which sets us free. In him, we find freedom to love others like he does.
Watch today’s video for an overview of Paul’s message to the Galatians and how the Gospel makes all things new.
Reflection Questions
Read Acts 15 and the Jerusalem Council. Why do you suppose Peter and others started to act differently around the antagonistic group despite the Council’s decision? Why do you think this creates considerable frustration for Paul?
Read Acts 9 and Paul’s commission from Jesus. How might the Galatians feel knowing that God specifically chose Paul to bring them the good news? How might this impact their current understanding of the debate raging in Galatia over the competing Gospel messages?

In this letter to Galatia, Paul is keenly aware of the power a fake gospel has to attract and destroy. Paul views these as high-stakes, life-and-death truths. So he amplifies his attempts from persuasion to provocation. In today’s reading, Paul faces the Galatians head-on with a hard question:
How could you be so easily duped?
To expose the false gospel infiltrating Galatian churches, Paul reminds them of two key stories, their own and Abraham’s. When you received the Holy Spirit, was it because you believed or because you followed human-created rules?
When Abraham was called “right” or “righteous” in his relationship with God, was it fundamentally because he
believed
God or because he was circumcised and therefore considered well-behaved by religious leaders?
Note: For Paul, belief connects to behavior inseparably, so he is
not
saying behavior matters less than belief. Instead, he’s suggesting that the truly faithful one orients on trust in God alone, not trust in the approval of religious “authorities.” Right standing before God comes through Jesus,
not
works of the law.
Next, Paul anticipates their objections: Paul, you say that righteousness comes by faith,
not
by following works of law, but you just quoted the law to make your point. So you’re really affirming the goodness of law-following. The antagonists you warn against do the same thing. So what makes your message different, really?
Paul explains that the law was good and served like a teacher or guardian (Gal. 3:24), exposing sin and showing God’s people how to live. However, the law was always temporary, serving as a guide to help people until the time Christ arrived.
Christ Jesus creates a permanent covenant that welcomes not some, but all people, into the family of God—into
his own life
(Gal. 3:28).
Notice how today’s reading begins with strong provocation but ends with patient, loving care. Paul might have a mighty pen, but it comes from a paternal heart.
Reflection Questions
Compare today’s reading to Jesus’ testimony in John 5:31-47. Where do you see Paul picking up on Jesus’ insistence that the Hebrew Scriptures point to and find their fulfillment in Jesus?
Look up Isaiah 49:15 and 66:13. What do these images of a mother’s fierce, committed love for her children reveal about Paul’s love for the Galatians and God’s love for his people?

If you’re set free from the law, does that mean you’re free to be lawless? No, says Paul, this is not freedom
from
restraint. It’s freedom
to love
. In Christ, you’re set free to live a specific way, compelled by love for God and neighbor.
In today’s reading, Paul explains that although the Galatians (and all people) are no longer required to follow the letter of Jewish law, they are free (and still instructed by God) to live in the spirit of that law. They can fulfill the law’s goal of loving one another without strictly adhering to its unique works, especially the cultural identifiers like food, Sabbath, and circumcision regulations.
Many Galatians remain concerned about how any person, especially a Gentile, could even understand God’s will without the law. So Paul essentially argues that one does not need Leviticus or Deuteronomy to discern that punching your neighbor, stumbling out of bars, or using and abusing others to satisfy lustful appetites opposes the spirit of God’s law. Living like that is rejecting Jesus’ Kingdom values.
The Spirit leads people to a better way, always. This leading includes energizing human beings to deal kindly with annoying or oppositional people, to deny the desires of the flesh, and to seek reconciliation when relationships break.
Still, some in Galatia were no doubt nervous about doing anything that looked like dishonoring God’s law. So Paul offers this final nugget: If you want to fulfill the law, carry one another’s burdens.
In other words, if you want to fulfill God’s law to its core, stop assuming that circumcision, eating kosher, or resting on the Sabbath are
themselves
the path to such fulfillment. Whether you’re still following those laws or not, the essence of God’s law is love. Fulfilling it means loving one another without prejudice or partiality.
The law was a guardian until Christ arrived. Christ showed the world that to fulfill all of God’s law is to love one another sincerely.
Reflection Questions
Take a moment to reread Matthew 16:5-12. How does Jesus use the metaphor of yeast to instruct his disciples in this story? How might this help you understand Paul’s use of the same metaphor to describe false teaching in the Galatian churches (Gal. 5:9)?
Compare Galatians 5:14 to Matthew 22:36-40. What have you learned from Paul and Jesus about the ultimate purpose of the law?

If you cruise by Psalm 8 too quickly, what you read might tempt you to go on a power trip. Humans crowned with glory and majesty? Yes, please. Bring me my scepter and loyal subjects. I get to be in charge!
But by looking closer and seeing Psalm 8 in context, as you’ll see in today’s video, you see the psalmist declaring that all things are actually subjected under
the messiah’s
feet. Humanity is ruling, but people rule in partnership with the messiah, meaning humanity alone is not in charge.
God created humans not to rule on their own, however they choose, but to bear his image and rule with God in God’s specific way. Today’s reading in Ephesians describes this kind of rulership with different “p” words:
praise
for God and the kind intention of his will,
prayer
for the Spirit-infused power to love God and others, and
people
uniting peacefully as portable temples.
That kind of ruling is about mutually blessing one another, not controlling one another.
Writing to the Ephesian church, Paul opens by exalting Jesus as the Messiah, whom Psalm 8 and the rest of the Hebrew Bible had long anticipated. Then, Paul prays that the Ephesians would come to realize that the Father sent the Messiah—his divine Son (not divine warriors)—to defeat evil oppressors. To be an evil-defeating cosmic ruler, therefore, is to live “in Christ,” a phrase Paul uses no less than 36 times in Ephesians alone.
The Messiah’s victory results not in God’s people experiencing infinite power to rule over their enemies with control, but in infinite power to live according to the way of God’s loving Spirit.
So Psalm 8 is absolutely correct. Yes, God enthroned humanity as his ruling partner. But such power neither subjugates nor divides. This power bears God’s image and presence in the world by walking in the way God first designed humanity.
For Paul, understanding the Messiah’s story allows the Ephesian church to embrace its own story. The Messiah grants power to participate in prayer, praise, and peace.
Reflection Questions
Paul tells the Ephesians that God created them to do “good works.” Meditating upon the whole Bible, what sorts of activities are good works? What people in the Scriptures modeled good works well?
Reflect on the purpose of the temple in the Hebrew Bible. What does it mean that the new humanity in Christ is now a temple? What role does this new temple play in the world?

Ephesians begins with power, exaltation, and people being seated in the heavenly realms. These are strong words from a man who is writing this letter while imprisoned. Don’t Paul’s chains undermine his entire message? How is he seated on high while also bound in chains down low? In today’s reading, Paul upends every expectation. He says his chains validate his claims.
Paul recognizes that imprisonment would be a source of shame and a sign of defeat to the Ephesians (and pretty much everyone). So he rethinks his shackles. He says that the new humanity in Christ gains its power not from government or religious authorities but from God’s Spirit. Real freedom in this sense cannot be taken away, even by oppressive rules and prison shackles.
After all, if Paul had political might or social capital, then his message might look a lot like a message repeated by this world’s strongest empires. But the good news of God now revealed in Christ marches forward not through those favored and granted “freedom” by the world’s powerful. It marches forward through loving people often ostracized by mainstream society—the poor, the lowly, and the despised, usually regarded as anything but free.
While chained up in a humble cell, Paul continues to freely spread the Gospel, knowing that God’s power is sufficient for the evil-defeating good works God calls each person to do.
What are those good works? Watch today’s video for a preview of how Paul argues that in light of the great salvation in the Messiah, the new humanity experiences transformation to live as a united body living out a new story.
Reflection Questions
Instead of being a status of shame, Paul considers his imprisonment to be for the glory of the Ephesians (Eph. 3:13). Paul finds his greatest honor in his suffering and ability to share and participate in Jesus’ suffering. In what ways is this different or similar to other value systems you observe around you?
Meditate upon Ephesians 4:1-3. How can humility, gentleness, patience, and love help maintain the unity of peace in Jesus? What happens to peace when one of these virtues is missing?

The battle between light and darkness begins, in a sense, at Genesis 1 when God’s created light pierces into the void. The unfolding story keeps promising that light always wins. Darkness cannot overcome it. Paul employs the light metaphor to encourage the Ephesians to walk the radiant road now, as children of light (Eph. 5:8-10).
For those who are new to the path, Paul has some signposts to point out that will help everyone along the way. For starters, those on the path embrace wisdom. It’s almost like Paul is appealing to the book of Proverbs and its insistence on following Lady Wisdom, not Lady Folly. People walking in God’s light will be guided by the Spirit, not guided by their physical impulses for pleasure and strong substances.
Even more, as you walk with those on the illuminated path, you’ll likely hear the pleasant hum of grateful songs, praising God. You’ll hear constant thank yous that build people up, encouraging words that bring life. Walking in the light leads to a wiser path and more lovely speech.
It also means new ways of relating to others as family and community. Paul transforms the standard household codes of his day, based on social rank, into a way of mutual submission, honoring subordinates and loving all without reservation.
Love and light go hand in hand for God’s people.
Walking in the light is wonderful, but Paul also cautions readers against presuming that the path will be easy. The Satan still lurks. He’s always deceiving and dragging people back into darkness, Paul reminds the Ephesians in closing. As you’ll see in today’s video, the Satan isn’t
for
anything. He’s
anti
-everything.
So be wise. Stand guard. Put on your spiritual armor. Allow the light to keep overcoming the darkness as it has been doing, nonstop, since the beginning.
Reflection Questions
Instead of sexual immorality, greed, and foolish talking, Paul says to have “grateful thanksgiving.” How does practicing gratitude counter these things?
Meditate upon the armor listed in Ephesians 6:10-18. How have you seen those pieces of armor used throughout the Scriptures? How did they help withstand the evil one?

When Paul planted the Philippian church, which we saw in Acts 16, those impacted by the Gospel would not have been social acquaintances, much less friends: a female God-fearer, a male gentile jailer, and an enslaved girl recently possessed by an evil spirit. These folks would have naturally avoided one another.
The unifying good news of Jesus was stronger than the mainstream social expectations driving them apart. Still, as members of this community learn to love one another, those pressures to divide based on social pride and ethnic tension linger on. It’s no surprise then that Paul emphasizes throughout this letter a way of humility that keeps building toward complete unity.
Remember that Paul is writing from a humble place here. He’s not directing lowly subjects from on high. Paul penned this letter while imprisoned, a space reserved for the socially humiliated.
See, especially in Philippians 2, how Paul regards Jesus as the ultimate example of humility. Jesus refused to grasp divine power to control people, instead choosing to become a servant who takes care of people (Phil. 2:6-7). Paul poetically illustrates Jesus’ unwavering willingness to serve while staying obedient to the Father’s will, obedience even to the point of willfully experiencing a gruesome crucifixion.
Jesus, the God-man deserving of
all
glory, nevertheless paid no attention to social rank when serving others. Paul encourages the Philippians to live the same way. He explains that following Jesus into a way of humility right now will
for sure
lead to exaltation later. The world may label humility as weak or foolish, assuming strength and dominance are more “glorious.” But for God in Christ, humility
is
most glorious. One day, everyone on Earth will agree that Jesus and the humble way of Christ-likeness will be exalted.
Watch today’s video to see how Paul shapes the letter of Philippians around the example of Jesus as the perfect model for unity and humility.
Reflection Questions
What hyperlinks to the Hebrew Bible do you notice in the messianic poem (Phil. 2:6-11)? What do those links reveal about Jesus’ identity?
While Jesus offers the best example of humility, love, and obedience, Paul also provides two other examples: Timothy and Epaphroditus. How do those two men serve as positive examples for the Philippian church? In what ways are they imitating Christ?

If you were imprisoned under Rome’s imperial guard and knew they might soon take your life, how would you describe your mood? What if you were deeply concerned about the faith communities you planted because of internal and external threats? Would you feel anxious? Forlorn?
What about
joyful?
That seems like a stretch.
It’s true for Paul, though. Despite his imprisonment and ongoing threats to his life, not to mention the attacks upon those he deeply loves, Paul saturates his letter to the Philippians with reminders that he remains joyful. He invites the Philippians to share in that joy.
At first, Paul’s proclamation of joy sounds surprising at best and like sentimental positivity at worst. However, when you gain a deeper understanding of joy from a biblical perspective, everything changes. You’ll learn more about biblical joy in today’s video and discover that it is not dependent on one’s circumstances, but is grounded in God’s promised future. Joy from that perspective becomes reasonable no matter what occurs.
So Paul ends his letter the same way he began, urging unity through humility. What better ingredient to bring folks together than rejoicing?
Paul’s letter to the Philippians is deeply personal and painful, with joy jumping off the pages at the same time that suffering seeps through the lines. Although it’s a relatively short letter, this combination of sorrow and jubilation makes Philippians a powerful reminder of how God’s people can experience true, good life even during this painful time before Christ comes and makes all things new.
Reflection Questions
Joy can persist in the harshest of circumstances because it depends on God and God’s promises. Read Matthew 5:11-12 and Acts 13:50-52. According to these passages and today’s reading, what specific truths about God can sustain joy even through painful or dire situations?
Notice how Paul describes the gift from the Philippians (4:17-18). Compare his language to Exodus 29:18 and Ezekiel 20:41. What does this reveal about the priesthood of all believers and their financial gifts?

While suffering Roman imprisonment, Paul gets word that Christians in Colossae are under heavy pressure, so he sends a hug and wise guidance with this letter. The Colossians are largely still strangers to Paul, but he knows they share his same love for Jesus and doesn’t hesitate to help.
Agitators have been destroying community peace, driving Jesus followers toward an appealing solution. Rather than trusting
only
Jesus,
also
trust the necessary, equally important human traditions that make you right with God, things like observing proper food and holy day regulations.
Be careful, says Paul, to avoid hollow philosophies that deceive, and beware the “human traditions” and “elemental spirits” that are
not according to Christ
(Col. 2:8). Paul’s opening poem about Christ’s supremacy makes his major point: There is one God, in Christ, who alone brings peace and right relationship with God (Col. 1:15-20). Living according to Christ’s way of loving neighbors? That’s trusting the true peacemaker, and it heals division.
Notice in Paul’s letters that when Christians face challenges to their faith, he reorients them to Jesus’ way rather than new strategies for defeating opponents. He’s reminding them that, above all else, conflicts resolve when people prioritize Jesus’ loving way of life.
Everything you need is in Christ, Paul promises. When others threaten you with terrible consequences for failing to observe human traditions around food, circumcision, and Sabbath regulations, fear not. Those threats are hollow. Through Jesus, we will fulfill every righteous requirement God has given.
Watch today’s video for an overview of Colossians, what it means that Christ is exalted, and how that impacts every area of our lives.
Reflection Questions
Meditate on the Messiah poem (Col. 1:15-20). What hyperlinks do you notice with the Hebrew Bible? What do those links reveal about Jesus?
Think back to Acts 15 and the Jerusalem Council. What decisions did the early church make about circumcision, food laws, and holy days? Why did they make those decisions? How does that impact your understanding of Paul’s teaching in Colossians 2:6-33?

Every day is a new-creation day for Paul. He believes Jesus will someday transform all of Heaven and Earth, ushering every galaxy, planet, person, cell, and atom into a new-creation reality with all people embracing the way of Christ. Why wait to start living like that now?
For Paul, Jesus’ resurrection kicked off the new creation. Humanity can enjoy that newness today. It’s a gift.
Today’s video and reading engage with what it means to live as the new humanity. Rooted in love and humility, the new humanity has little in common with a world where survival is for the fittest, with old ways of competition and prioritizing oneself over one’s neighbor.
The old way bathes in anger, blasts out slander, and bellies up to the bar for every evil desire. Jesus’ new way is clothed in compassion, speaking with softness, and mutually loving those who the world calls “least.” Jesus’ new humanity brings Heaven to Earth. Every decision to follow in his way glimpses that new creation. In Christ, all things are new—new speech, new desires, new family structures, new priorities, new humans.
Though Paul never meets the Colossians in person, he is confident these new friends in Christ will respond well to the loving and liberating rule of Jesus.
Reflection Questions
Consider the list of new humanity virtues Paul exhorts the Colossians to imitate: merciful, kind, humble, gentle, patient, forbearing, forgiving, and loving. What concrete behaviors demonstrate these virtues?
A new humanity results in new households transformed by the Lordship of Jesus. How do these new households differ from some of the households mentioned in the Hebrew Bible? How does Jesus’ model for self-giving love transform old ways of relating as a household?

Paul makes enemies during his missionary journeys. Lots of enemies. When he stops in Thessalonica, his teaching sparks an actual riot in the city center. Loud, condemning voices accuse Paul and his companions of turning the whole world upside down. Paul and his co-laborers escape under the cloak of darkness to avoid further danger.
At their next stop, while enjoying generous hospitality and robust curiosity from the Bereans, Paul and Silas suddenly find themselves surrounded by angry rabble-rousers who followed them from Thessalonica. Like fire, anger travels fast, putting Paul and company in mortal danger.
Forced once again to flee, Paul feels skyrocketing concern and anguish for the young, fledgling church in Thessalonica. He wants to know how they’re doing. Can they survive this persecution? Will they remain faithful to Jesus?
Imagine Paul’s delight when Timothy reports back. The Thessalonians remain strong despite the opposition! Relieved, grateful, and proud, Paul pens this personal letter to celebrate the Thessalonians and encourage them to continue in their pursuit of holiness and love.
Watch today’s video to get an overview of this deeply personal and celebratory letter, written to a small but mighty group of believers.
Reflection Questions
Paul’s first letter to the Thessalonian church begins with a recognition of the recipients’ faithfulness. Notice how Paul highlights their faith, love, and hope. What does this reveal about the role of celebration and encouragement in Christian life?
The Thessalonians turned from idols to the
living and true
God. How does that description parallel the way the Hebrew Bible discusses idols? See Isaiah 44:9-20 and Jeremiah 10:1-16 for examples.

If yesterday Paul was the cheerleader for the Thessalonians’ faithfulness, today he puts down the pom-poms and picks up the whistle. Coach Paul is ready to motivate this church toward more comprehensive faithfulness. His mantra? Do even more. More holiness. More love. More hope. These things cannot be overdone.
Before the Thessalonians walked with Jesus, they worshiped idols and practiced the sexual norms of their day. Walking with Jesus is about truly loving other people. Jesus’ way naturally discourages sexual impurity because that’s a way of using people rather than loving them. Paul aims to help his friends pursue holiness, which is the good life of Christ.
Paul reasons that controlling one’s body also prevents harming your brothers and sisters. Turning from idols means turning from practices that deceive and enslave. You’ve got this, my beloved Thessalonians— more restraint from evil means more freedom to be truly loving, truly human, and truly
alive.
Paul affirms that he already sees the Thessalonians loving one another well. They’re showing up to practice and faithfully completing their drills. Like any good coach, he sees championship potential in this team, and he wants to fan their bright spark into a brilliant flame.
Why? In a word:
hope
. We pursue greater holiness and love because we have living hope in a greater future, a world where God’s goodness saturates all creation. Christ is returning and will ultimately make all things new. So keep encouraging one another on the good path.
Paul celebrates the Thessalonians while also acknowledging that people walking with Christ need continuous encouragement. On Jesus’ path, things like holiness, love, and hope cannot be overdone. Keep loving, Paul says, more and more and more.
Reflection Questions
Yesterday, we meditated on the role of celebration in the Christian life. Today, Paul challenges the Thessalonians to pursue greater holiness, love, and hope. What does this reveal about the role of challenge and encouragement in the Christian life?
Paul’s emphasis on holiness, love, and hope echoes the words of Jesus in the Gospels. How did Jesus talk about these virtues? What does this reveal about the priorities of those who belong to Jesus’ Kingdom?

Paul has been celebrating the Thessalonians’ faithfulness with streamers and balloons. However, his sincere hope does not minimize his grief over the persecution his friends must suffer under Roman rule. They could use some warm affection. So Paul ends his letter with kind, fatherly advice.
First, he tells them how to face bullies. He pokes fun at the
Pax Romana
, or “peace of Rome,” a term used to boast about Rome’s ability to achieve peace through strength. Empire stability resulted, but only through violent force. Tyranny like that will someday return to overtake them.
Hyperlinking to language from the prophets, Paul reminds his friends that the Day of the Lord will be a time of reckoning for oppressors and a time of victory for the oppressed. To Paul, the
Pax Romana
was more like the fake “peace” Israel’s prophets poked fun at when they said, “Peace, peace, when there is no peace!” (see Jer. 6:14, 8:11; Ezek. 13:16; Mic. 3:5). The prophets also said tyranny always ends up destroying the tyrannical.
That bully of yours will not be allowed to bully forever (and never truly wins), so find peace in Christ rather than the strength to topple bullies. Want to end the tyranny of bullies throughout the world? Jesus does as well. Paul teaches his friends to stick with Jesus’ strategy, which means doing it differently. Help the weak. Pursue good. Rejoice always. Be careful to stay away from every kind of evil.
To close, Paul blesses his beloved. “May the God of peace make you entirely holy, and may your spirit, soul, and body be kept completely blameless” (1 Thess. 5:23). In other words, like a loving father might pray for his own family, Paul asks Christ to watch over and protect his spiritual children.
Following Jesus is tough. Sometimes you will have to flee in the middle of the night and face depressing hardships. Paul’s first letter to the Thessalonians reminds everyone who reads it that those who walk with God will need cheering, challenge, and the kind of comfort only God can give.
Reflection Questions
Notice the parallels between Isaiah 59:17 and Paul’s instructions to the Thessalonians to put on the armor. What might this hyperlink reveal about the connection between Jesus followers and the suffering servant of Isaiah?
Meditate on Paul’s final instructions for the Thessalonians (5:12-22). If a community follows these instructions, what will its day-to-day life together look like? How does it reveal Paul’s fatherly affections for this church (2:11)?

Through deep suffering, deep love is often born. God has been helping the Thessalonian church through a painful transformation into a community compelled by love. They have endured the violence of mobs, the loss of beloved pastors when Paul and Silas fled by night, the death of loved ones, and threats of imprisonment.
During hardship, Timothy first reports strong faithfulness in the community. Now, a second report says that as the persecution mounts, things turn for the worse.
A counterfeit Paul has been hawking the message that Jesus has already returned, meaning that he has abandoned the Thessalonians. Paul rejects such claims and exhorts the Thessalonians: Don’t be easily shaken or disturbed by any spirit or message or letter about God’s justice that allegedly came from me or any other true apostle (2 Thess. 2:2).
Paul comforts the church by reminding them that someday Jesus will return and deal justly with those who now deal in deception and violence. Then, he reminds them that evil kingdoms and empires are nothing new. The oppression of rulers is an ancient problem. Rome will crumble as Jesus returns, just like all empires that dominate through fear and harm.
And don’t worry. When Jesus returns, it will be loud and lovely. You won’t be able to miss it!
Watch the video to see how this message of Jesus’ future return informs how believers live today. Jesus’ return inspires hope, motivates justice, and comforts the afflicted.
Reflection Questions
Read Acts 17 and the beginning of the Thessalonian church. How does their origin prepare them for their current suffering?
Think back on how the prophets describe the Day of the Lord (Jer. 46:10; Ezek. 30:2-3; Joel 1; Amos 5). How do those passages reveal that the Day of the Lord will be good news for God’s people? How does this alleviate fears about Jesus’ future return?

Usually, it’s frowned upon to read someone else’s mail. But Paul would warmly encourage us to read his letters, as long as we remember that Paul is not writing
to
us. Instead, these letters are
for
us. Paul gave his letters to the church for the development of wisdom. Jesus followers have long read the New Testament’s letters from the apostles as Scripture. For each generation of believers, the goal is to live in that wisdom in their own day, in their own way.
Letters like Paul’s to the Roman, Colossian, Corinthian, or Thessalonian church focus on an entire community. In 1 and 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon, Paul speaks more intimately to individuals. Previously, Paul sent a young pastor, Timothy, to Ephesus to clean up a theological and preaching mess.
Between crowds chanting for Artemis (a female deity that most Ephesians worshiped), corrupt teachers making falsehood popular, and chaotic families constantly disrupting the church, young Timothy has had his work cut out for him. So Paul sends a strong, encouraging message to his beloved junior colleague: Fight the good fight in the good way of Christ.
Fight like Christ? Battle lines and battalions give way to the power of true faith when fighting the good fight. This will look like praying for all people, including corrupt kings in authority, so that the power of God and humble, loving human life can ultimately prevail. Rather than “fight opposers wherever you find them,” Paul says to “pray in every place, lifting up holy hands without anger or dispute” (1 Tim. 2:8).
Paul tells Timothy that the goal of his instruction should be merciful love, not conquest. If beliefs give way to behavior, as Paul argues in this letter, then you can win the fight with the Gospel proclamation and love for your listeners. The fight for Ephesus is a battle of beliefs. We win the ground game through kindness and care.
Watch today’s video to preview the ways Paul encourages Timothy to lead Ephesus in becoming a church known for love of Jesus and neighbor.
Reflection Questions
Paul contends in this letter that what a community believes impacts how they will behave. How have you seen this displayed throughout the Bible? How did Jesus’ ministry reflect this truth?
Consider Paul’s admonition to live peaceful and quiet lives for the sake of the Gospel (1 Tim. 2:1-4). Why might Paul encourage this among the believers in Ephesus? Look back at Acts 19 for more context.

Ephesus is sick. The remedy prescribed by the Apostle Paul? Develop healthy leaders who publicly proclaim the living God with boldness.
In today’s reading, Paul instructs Timothy to recruit, evaluate, and appoint faithful leaders to help him nurse Ephesus back to health. Rather than viewing the qualifications for church leadership as a universal checklist for all times and places, think of them as Paul’s picture of a mature person living and speaking in the culture of 1st-century Ephesus.
Belligerent bullies might seem strong, but choose the true strength we see in Jesus by choosing gentle, wise guides. Paul reasons that if a person leads a flourishing home, they can lead a flourishing church. Disease is notorious for spreading, but health can spread even more.
After appointing healthy leaders, Paul says, Timothy must attack the illness at its source: twisted theology. False, damaging ideas about family genealogies, meat regulations, and marriage customs have spread throughout the city. To counter these claims, Paul encourages Timothy to practice the public reading of Scripture.
Like Moses at Mount Sinai, like Joshua about to enter the promised land, like King Josiah rediscovering and reading the Torah publicly, and like Ezra and Nehemiah after the exile, Timothy joins a long line of leaders who shepherd God’s people through the communal reading of God’s good word.
The story we belong to suggests that this way of fighting evil worked well for Israel. Paul seems to think the same approach should work well for Ephesus. He wants to see Ephesus thrive in the way of Christ, which happens best in partnership with others. So he talks about sending in helpers to encourage Timothy and his co-laborers in the way of Scripture-guided ministry, modeled by God’s past faithful leaders.
Reflection Questions
Meditate on the picture of healthy leaders in chapter 3. How do these qualities demonstrate maturity?
Paul encourages Timothy to devote himself to the public reading of Scripture along with exhortation and teaching. How do other leaders practice the public reading of Scripture in the Hebrew Bible? (For a hint, look back at Deuteronomy 31:9-13, Joshua 8:34-35, and 2 Kings 23:1-3.) Why might this be an important practice for God’s people?

Some say money is the root of all evil, but money
itself
is neither good nor bad. Paul says more specifically that it is humanity’s “love of money” that causes evil (1 Tim. 6:10). We noted in yesterday’s reading that the city of Ephesus is sick. This sickness makes it challenging to develop a healthy church community within it. Today’s reading exposes one root cause of that sickness: love for money.
Ephesus boasted big bucks as a coastal city with active trading ports, bustling businesses, natural resources, and settled elites, its wealth shimmering on the Mediterranean. But immense wealth fuels greed and often creates an unjust (yet familiar) scenario where some have more than imaginable while others have less than necessary.
To save money and keep building wealth, some families are abandoning widowed mothers by withholding financial support instead of caring for them as God has always prescribed. Desperate, these women are gleaning so much financial support from the church that it has become burdensome.
Worse, Paul says, false teachers who love money are performing godly-looking deeds in public, not to help anyone, but to benefit themselves and satisfy their greed. Worse yet, love for money seduces wealthy church members, their hope less in Jesus and more in wealth for future security.
The obsession with material resources does not create a just, flourishing social world, but a miserable situation in Ephesus. To stave off greed, Paul instructs Timothy to lead people away from loving money by encouraging contentment and a love for generosity. Right relationships matter more than anything, so use money to strengthen social bonds.
If Ephesus is famous for its wealth, then the Ephesian church has the opportunity to be world-renowned for its generosity. The love of money might be the root of all evil, but love for others through generous sharing is the root of righteousness.
Watch today’s video to see how Paul’s teaching on generosity flows directly from Jesus’ teaching on righteousness in the Sermon on the Mount.
Reflection Questions
How does Paul’s teaching on caring for family and giving of money in today’s reading parallel Jesus’ teaching in the Sermon on the Mount?
Meditate on Paul’s closing words (1 Tim. 6:15-16). How do these words contrast against the false worship of pagan gods and idols? Why might these be the last words chosen for this letter?

Shipwrecked, snakebitten, flogged, shackled, beaten, and now shivering in a Roman prison, Paul has endured tremendous pain—suffering he had expected since meeting the crucified and risen Jesus. To join the mission of God’s suffering servant, Paul knew that he, like Jesus, would “suffer hardship to the point of imprisonment as a criminal” (2 Tim 2:8). And he knows that Timothy has been facing the same.
For guidance, Paul says to Timothy, turn to the Scriptures your grandmother and mother taught you. Paul knows he’ll need them. Also, remember that God’s Spirit is not timid. “God did not give us a Spirit of fear, but instead, power, love, and self-restraint” (2 Tim. 1:7).
Be courageous. Stay true to God’s instruction. As a Hebrew Bible scholar, Paul certainly knew that God once told Joshua the same thing when Joshua faced his greatest challenge. Be strong and “very courageous,” God said, and follow the Torah carefully. “Do not turn to the left or the right from it, so that your way may go well in all that you do” (Josh. 1:7). Paul conveys the same good directives to Timothy, who also faces his greatest challenge.
Paul knows how to suffer well. He knows how to endure shame without being chained to it, and not only because he’s had plenty of practice. Paul anchors into the long biblical pattern of finding God’s wisdom in God’s word. He’s helping Timothy find and trust God the same way.
Paul invites his protégé to let go of the painful yet temporary stigma of social shame by clinging to the Savior instead. Lead your church, Timothy, by prioritizing the faithful teaching of the Scriptures and trusting God to guide your steps. Watch the video to see how Paul weaves these themes together, exhorting Timothy to ground his life in God’s reality by trusting in his word.
At the end of Paul’s life, alone in a Roman prison, despite all the trauma, grief, abandonment, and shame, he says to Timothy, “I know the Lord will bring me home safely” (2 Tim. 4:18) and ends the letter with a blessing for Timothy and all who read it. “Grace be with you” (2 Tim. 4:22).
Reflection Questions
Take a moment to reflect on Paul’s life. How does his life reflect that of Jesus? What does this reveal about the role of suffering in the life of God’s people?
Despite being abandoned by his friends, Paul knows Jesus stands by his side. Who else in the Scriptures felt abandoned by others but received the comfort of God? What does this reveal about the enduring love of God?

Sending this letter to Titus on the island of Crete, Paul describes Cretan culture in much the same way the prophets described idolatrous culture. When worshiping a god carved from a dead log, Isaiah said, you end up like the log—dumb and dead (Isa. 44:9-20). Statues can’t see, speak, or hear, Jeremiah said, so worshiping them blinds and deafens you to reality (Jer. 10:3-5, 8-15).
People become
like
the gods they worship, the things they behold. Beholding something is to see or observe a thing or person, especially a remarkable or impressive one. Here, Paul tells Titus that those who worship Zeus—a lying, cheating, dominating god—end up lying, cheating, and fighting like the average Cretan was renowned for.
Thankfully, Paul sent Titus along with this letter to encourage the Cretans to behold, and therefore become like, a seeing, breathing, living God. Watch the video to see how Paul’s letter to Titus outlines his strategy for helping people get to know Jesus on the island of Crete, a wild place known for a culture of heavy drinking and brutish, carnal behavior.
Calling for neither retreat nor confrontation, Paul encourages the Cretan Christians to worship the Messiah and become like him, to live with every neighbor in the way of Jesus. Unlike Zeus, Jesus has no deceit in his mouth—the only thing Jesus cheats is death—and Jesus protects and renews all life. So to behold (and thus become like)
this
God, Jesus, is to enter his life, which even death cannot end.
So live anew, Paul teaches Titus. Step out of the exciting yet pointless controversies that people become stuck in. Leave behind the anger, violence, and medicating tactics like heavy drinking that have become customary. Instead, be kind, generous, self-controlled, and faithful to God’s instruction. To help a whole community follow Jesus, appoint wise leaders, build new households, and together become like Jesus. It’s a new way of being human.
Because of Christ, Paul reminds Titus, the “grace of God has been revealed, bringing salvation to all people” (Tit. 2:11). Not just to a favorite people—Paul says to
all
people. That gift, or grace, compels us to live rightly with others, with love and self-control rather than carnal brutality.
Reflection Questions
Meditate on Paul’s missionary strategy for pursuing the common good of their neighbors. How does this relate to Jeremiah’s message during exile and Daniel’s message of peaceful resistance?
We become what we behold. How does Paul envision the new humanity that beholds Jesus will behave toward their Jesus following community? Toward outsiders? What implications does this have for Jesus followers today?

Imagine working for a wealthy man who legally owns you. Leaving him would also be considered stealing from him. As a slave, you cannot legally marry, you cannot legally own things, and you don’t even have legal authority over your own body—your master owns it. Imagine then, starving for your own freedom, you finally escape!
But the wise, trusted friend with whom you take refuge says, “Actually, you should go back.”
What?
“Oh, and please deliver this letter to your owner.”
Today, we’re reading Paul’s letter to Philemon, a household patriarch who owned slaves, as all Roman patriarchs did back then. Onesimus is a runaway slave and mail carrier. The letter paints a scene. We’re watching the Gospel drive roots into Rome’s cultural bedrock, disrupting every assumption about human value.
Philemon trusts Jesus. Paul writes not to criticize, but to build up. He wants to see Philemon’s instinctive allegiance to the Roman way transform into loving allegiance to Jesus and his way. According to Roman law, Onesimus harmed Philemon by running away, a punishable crime of theft. But Jesus’ law of love says Onesimus and Philemon are mutually one, united as true family.
Remember the famous Christmas song, "O, Holy Night"? “
Truly he taught us to love one another; his law is love, and his Gospel is peace. Chains shall he break, for the slave is our brother, and in his name, all oppression shall cease.”
That’s basically Paul’s letter in a nutshell. It’s brief but explosive. Paul is asking Philemon to welcome Onesimus home—not as a slave, but as
more than
a slave, as a brother. Modeling Jesus’ law of love for Philemon, Paul promises to fully repay any debt still owed by Onesimus for any harm done. This is the way.
This is what Gospel partners do. They recognize their mutuality before God—no person greater, no person lesser, equally one, and equally free in the Messiah. Today’s video explains more about Onesimus and Philemon’s backstory. It further explores the letter’s radical heartbeat born from a love that breaks chains, sees slaves become brothers, and makes all oppression cease.
Reflection Questions
Deuteronomy 23:15-16 prohibits returning a slave to his master. Why do you suppose Paul takes this route to bring Onesimus and Philemon back together?
Paul’s letter to Philemon shows that the good news of the Gospel creates more than private implications. It results in public changes, too. How does this bring about goodness and peace to the new humanity created by Jesus?

Who wrote the book of Hebrews? Some say Paul or Barnabas. Others say someone else. We’re not sure. Who are its first recipients? That’s fuzzy, too. The writing suggests that the original recipients belonged to a community under heavy persecution, having lost their founding pastors.
Increasing hostility toward Jesus followers is causing them to “drift away” (Heb. 2:1) and “shrink back” (Heb. 10:39). They are abandoning faith in God and instead trusting the protection of social isolation. If we dial down our Jesus talk and just do what everyone else is doing, maybe the mocking and harm will lighten up.
But Jesus did not shrink back or withdraw into the “safety” of isolation. And recall the apostle Paul teaching young Timothy that God did not call followers to a “spirit of timidity” (2 Tim. 1:7). With God’s long-established promise—the same promise he gave to many, all of whom still wait in faith for that promise to be entirely fulfilled (see Heb. 11)—followers have good reason to embrace the same courage they see in the Messiah.
Wanting readers to see the promise they belong to, the author weaves Hebrew Bible hyperlinks together as far as the eye can see. This long story about God’s ultimate victory is also
your
story. Don’t be afraid. Don’t shrink back. You belong to God most high, who we see most clearly in his Son, the Messiah (Heb. 1:1-4).
While reading, you will notice a pattern. First, the author will tout Jesus’ supremacy above all, which then becomes the grounds for sharp challenges to remain faithful. Jesus is the supreme revelation of God. He shows us what God is like, and he reigns above even the angels.
While these holy messengers once delivered God’s word on Sinai, they now bow to Jesus and serve him alone. This brings a strong admonition. If the inferior revelation of an angel’s message required obedience, how much more important is obedience to the ultimate revelation of God in Jesus?
Watch the video to see different literary techniques used by the author of Hebrews to elevate Jesus and spur the audience toward steadfast trust in God alone.
Reflection Questions
The author of Hebrews assumes the reader understands the Hebrew Bible and can therefore pick up on many direct citations, allusions, and other hyperlinks. See one example by first reading Hebrews 1:5, then its connected reference in Psalm 2:7, and then all of Psalm 2. How does seeing the larger context of Psalm 2 provide insight into Hebrews 1:5?
The warnings in Hebrews may feel uncomfortable (e.g., Heb. 6:1-12), but they aim to exhort readers to greater faithfulness. Where else in the Hebrew Bible have you seen harsh-sounding speech intended for good? Paul told the Corinthians that God is the “God of all comfort” (2 Cor. 1:3-5). However, both here in Hebrews and throughout the Bible, God repeatedly delivers unsettling, uncomfortable,
serious
warnings. What does all of this suggest about the
kind
of comfort God intends to give?

Of all God’s servants, Moses was the number one greatest prophet, leader, and lawgiver. But the author of Hebrews says,
even
Moses bows to the Messiah, God’s Son, the greatest servant of all.
Moses submits to another? Yes. Israel’s Pharaoh-confronting, Red-Sea-splitting, Torah-receiving, tabernacle-constructing, wilderness-leading, people-judging prophet and patriarch is only one part of a bigger story.
This bold statement is not to belittle Moses—not at all. The author praises Moses, referring to him as a “servant” with the Greek term
therapon
.
Therapon
suggests an honorable status for “one who renders devoted service.” And the author does not use the common term
doulos
, meaning “slave or servant,” which conveys a lower status.
Moses is the chief of God’s household. However, God builds the house and creates everything. So the builder, God in the Messiah, reigns over the household. Moses built the tabernacle according to a cosmic template, but Jesus built the entire cosmos, including that cosmic template.
Jesus surpasses all, Moses included. So it’s wise to trust and obey Jesus more than Israel’s wilderness generation trusted God when their knees were knocking at Kadesh Barnea, on the edge of the promised land. They were so terrified by their foes that they abandoned faith and lost the ability to see reality through God’s eyes.
They did whatever made the most sense in their own eyes and died in the wild (Num. 13-14). They rejected God’s promise and refused to enter the promised land. Even Moses died in the wilderness with them. “Learn from that mistake! Don’t let fear shake you now,” the author of Hebrews is saying.
Today’s final reading unit, Hebrews 4:14-16, is worth our close attention. There, the author begins an explanation (which continues into tomorrow’s reading) for why we can trust Jesus. He describes Jesus as being both God’s divine high priest
and
humanity’s flesh-and-blood brother at the same time.
He’s not an “other,” judging humanity from a safe distance. Rather, Jesus is united with humanity, suffering with us to provide merciful assistance—not condemnation—when we falter in humanity’s common weakness (Heb. 4:16). So walk boldly without fear, bowing in peace at Jesus’ throne. And trust Jesus all the way into his promise of new life in the new creation.
Reflection Questions
Thinking back to Numbers 13-14, why did the Israelites fail to enter the promised land? How does that shed light on the meaning of today’s passage in Hebrews?
Meditate on Hebrews 4:12-13. What conclusions can we draw about the word of God from these verses? What does this suggest about accountability to God’s revelation?

Throughout generations, Israel’s people depended on holy priests from the tribe of Levi to rise each morning, dress in linen garments, hear confessions, inspect sacrifices, slaughter animals, burn incense … wash, rinse, repeat. Once a year, the high priest could enter the temple’s most sacred space, where God met with humanity, the holy of holies, protected by a thick veil.
Then one day, a holier priest from Judah tore that temple veil wide open. He ended the necessity for Israel to continue its ancient sacrificial system.
Today, we’re reading about the superiority of Jesus’ priestly work over that of Aaron and every Levite priest. Each son of Aaron had to not only sacrifice animals daily on behalf of God’s people, but he also had to face the reality of his own weakness and failure. Aaron’s priests
themselves
needed atoning forgiveness.
Jesus, as God’s superior high priest, never fails to live according to God’s will. He therefore needs no atoning sacrifice like the other priests. Now, giving himself as the final atoning sacrifice for all, Jesus passed through the temple curtain, able to enter the holy of holies and bring humanity with him into union with God. Because he does this, people then become a new temple where God and humanity meet as one.
The author is delivering a cautionary question throughout today’s reading. Will you trust in this greater high priest and move forward with confidence in his ability to fully unite you with God? Or will you fall away, returning to a deadly overconfidence in your own abilities?
Reflection Questions
Hebrews argues that Jesus’ priesthood exceeds the Aaronic priesthood for many reasons. Read Leviticus 4 about the requirements for when a priest brings guilt upon the community. How does Jesus’ priesthood remove the need for continued sacrifice?
While the warning passages in Hebrews use strong language, the author also writes encouraging and optimistic reminders. Meditate on Hebrews 6. How does the author both warn, encourage, and inspire hope?

Digital screens allow people to stay home and view great works of art from around the world. But no screen can capture the experience of beauty and wonder one has when standing under Michelangelo’s work at the Sistine Chapel or seeing the textured paint swirls of van Gogh’s
Starry Night
in person.
Like the digital screen, the tabernacle built by Moses and the Israelites in the wilderness offers a beautiful representation of God’s ideal Heaven-on-Earth space. But nothing beats the real thing.
In today’s reading, the author calls the Mosaic tabernacle, served by the sons of Abraham, a “copy” and “shadow” of the true tabernacle served by Jesus. As you will see in the video, Jesus’ royal priesthood ushers in a new covenant and expands God’s sacred space to include
all
of creation. The sons of Abraham attended to a tent that pointed forward. Jesus attends to everywhere.
The prophet Jeremiah longed to see the day when exactly
this
would happen, the Day of the Lord, when all of creation would become sacred space (Jer. 31:31-34). The author of Hebrews tells us Jesus is leading that work, uniting Heaven and Earth together until every square inch of the universe can become sacred space.
But how?
Does “Jesus alone can lead this work” also mean that “Jesus alone can accomplish this work?”
Yes. Jeremiah spoke of God’s divinely empowered renewal, but notice where such renovation first begins—within the human heart. Jeremiah says, and the author of Hebrews famously repeats, that God’s renewal includes writing his instruction “on their hearts” (Heb. 8:10). As it turns out, God’s primary concern wasn’t whether people would obey him. He was transforming humanity into a people who
wanted
to obey him, people who had God’s law written on their hearts.
The old laws and old ways were good, but the law remained external, a rule to live by rather than a sincerely loving way to follow. The law on its own and Israel’s normal human weakness weren’t enough to stave off rebellion and hardened hearts, even as Levi’s priests worked so hard toward the opposite end.
Jesus’ priesthood is different, patterned after the enigmatic figure of Melchizedek: kingly, priestly, and eternal. Melchizedek was a famous exception to the normal priestly pattern. Jesus is now the ultimate exception, establishing God’s permanent covenant of love. The old way has ended, and the new way is much, much better.
Reflection Questions
Read the account of Melchizedek and Abraham in Genesis 14. How does that story shed light upon Jesus’ priesthood?
The author of Hebrews quotes Jeremiah 31. How does Jesus’ royal priesthood usher in this new covenant? What changes between the old and new covenants are especially important to the author of Hebrews?

Have you ever experienced peace so restful that you exhale, unclench your jaw, and say to yourself, “Ah, this is Heaven on Earth?” Those moments hint at a deep longing to be with God in the Heaven that we can all share. We may not state, “I want to be with God in Heaven,” but the desires for lasting peace are, according to the Scriptures, the same desires to share sacred space with God while also feeling no shame or fear—fully relaxed and permanently at peace.
God’s space is all about true peace. The author of Hebrews reminds us that Jesus’ self-sacrifice makes it possible for everyone to enter that space. He refers to the clear conscience Jesus creates in us through his all-encompassing, once-and-for-all sacrifice, which not only purifies
us
but removes sin
completely
(Heb. 9:14).
This removal of sin makes it possible for every person to stand before God without guilt, anxiety, or feelings of unworthiness. The supremacy of Jesus’ sacrifice enables every human being to draw near to God confidently. For anyone in this letter’s original audience, Jewish and Gentile alike, it’s a wild claim.
For faithful Jewish worshipers, drawing near to God depended on the Levitical sacrifice system. Nearness required bloodshed over, and over, and over again. The animal’s blood brought enough purification to enter God’s space. However, God did not design the animal sacrifices to permanently cleanse them for ongoing, peaceful rest with him. Jesus’ blood provides that.
Jesus was good news for Gentiles as well. The Bible is full of stories about non-Israelites with their own temples, sacrifices, and systems built to honor gods that they trusted for prosperity, justice, and ultimately the same peace everyone longs for. These systems likewise could not bring lasting peace.
The supremacy of Jesus’ sacrifice means that there is no place God dwells that we are unworthy to be. So then, the author encourages his audience to
boldly
draw near to God “with a sincere heart,” free from shame and fear, and able to spur one another on toward love and good works (Heb. 10:22-24). Because of Jesus, we are already one with God.
Reflection Questions
Reflect on what kinds of actions or substances made a person ritually unclean in the Hebrew Bible. (For a refresher, read Leviticus 13-15.) How do these previously required purification rituals inform your understanding of Jesus’ all-encompassing sacrifice and purifying work?
God wants to be with his people. Reflect upon the whole story of Scripture. What are a few other ways that you have seen God draw near to his people? What does this suggest about God’s character or affection?

Public shaming, intense persecution, and threats of imprisonment all make people hypervigilant, seeing danger at every turn and becoming fearful. They need encouragement that even when pain blinds, making it a struggle to see what’s most true, Jesus is still worth following. After spending several chapters arguing for the supremacy of Jesus, the letter pivots. It encourages readers to follow Jesus by faith, not by sight.
Chapter 11 envisions a Hebrew Bible hall of fame for faithful women and men who came before. By faith, Noah built a floating ark on dry ground with no lake in sight. By faith, Moses’ family hid him from the murderous Pharaoh. By faith, Rahab welcomed the Israelite spies and trusted that a red cord would save her family.
All of these faithful examples faced persecution, ridicule, and death threats. They trusted God’s promise more than their senses, and God delivered them.
But
it’s also true that they died and stayed dead, which means everyone “commended for their faith” still has yet to “receive what was promised” (Heb. 11:39).
The point is that if we think our hardship proves God is untrustworthy, that he hasn’t kept his promise, remember people like Moses who felt the same way and stayed faithful. God has not abandoned them or you, and the promise is still good. We’re all still waiting for its complete fulfillment.
After looking back, the author encourages the audience to look up at the cross. You are suffering like God’s Son suffered, which makes you a child of God. As you’ll see in today’s video, Jesus calls those who face persecution not unfortunate but blessed ones.
The original audience got kidney punched with brutal, bloody hardship, and the disorienting pain made them shrink back, distrust God, and neglect Jesus’ great salvation. Return to ways of steadfast faith, says the author throughout the book of Hebrews, and fix your eyes on the absolute supremacy of Jesus, who is the author and perfecter of faith (Heb. 12:2).
Reflection Questions
People sometimes refer to Hebrews 11 as the “Hall of Faith.” Are there any people in the chapter that surprise you? What do these stories reveal about faith and the things in people that God focuses on the most?
Jesus’ beatitudes flip much of the world’s values upside down. How does Hebrews 11 align with Jesus’ teaching on blessedness? How does this comfort those who suffer?

As a genre title, “wisdom literature” sounds great. But it punches hard—delivering raw reality that rocks every faint heart. Grotesque suffering grounds Job’s wisdom story. Qoholet’s pained refrain calls all of life “
hevel, hevel!
”—“meaningless, meaningless!”—in Ecclesiastes. And while Proverbs is more upbeat, it repeatedly forecasts dismal destruction for those who deny wisdom. Now, take some hard-biting wisdom literature and add some of Jesus’ best material. That’s the book of James.
You’ll see in the video that James, also called Jacob (Greek:
Yacobos
, from the Hebrew:
Ya’akov
), opens with traditional greetings but quickly delves into punchy exhortation that reads more like collected Jesus-wisdom literature.
James details practical wisdom to guide right-relating ways of life, compelling the reader to care for their neighbors. If you say, “I have faith in God!” but then hoard wealth, neglect those who need assistance, and ignore God’s way, then your faith is dead. Not less. Not weak.
Dead
. Language like that feels like an elbow to the gut. You can’t
not
pay attention.
Chapter 1 introduces James’ main ideas for the letter, including themes such as tangibly loving others, caring for the poor, and sincere devotion to God. He populates the rest of his letter like a collection of mini-speeches, biting and clever, one-liner after another. Think of the book of James less like one of Paul’s letters and more like an anthology of aphorisms: short, truthful, wise teachings.
James exposes social favoritism as direct opposition to God (Jas. 2:1-13), which parallels Jesus’ teaching in Matthew 5:46-48. As Jesus taught and lived, the goal is to love each human being as a miraculous creation of God—
everyone
, not only those who deserve it. No human being has what it takes to judge another as unworthy of love or life.
So leave the judging to God. Keep on graciously blessing everyone. Then comes the zinger. “For judgment is merciless for the one who has shown no mercy. But mercy triumphs over judgment” (Jas. 2:13). Ouch—
and
amen. The sting serves the same purpose as each of James’ wisdom sayings and his entire letter: to prod God’s people toward wholehearted devotion to Jesus.
Reflection Questions
Meditate on James chapter 1. What parallels do you see to Proverbs? What about the Sermon on the Mount? What does this reveal about God’s enduring wisdom?
God gives wisdom to those who ask in faith (Jas. 1:5-8). Where in the Hebrew Bible have you seen this same idea communicated or this same thing take place? What could this mean for those who desire wisdom?

What is the goal of wisdom literature? Certainly, it aims to make one wise. When you see the fruit of wisdom, its deeper objective emerges. Wise living creates flourishing relationships. But James is not merely saying that wisdom benefits you. Wisdom benefits
everyone
by creating strong social bonds.
Today’s final sections of James offer another collection of proverbial sayings that focus on the way people speak, use wealth, and commit to the Lord. Again, James’ words are like barbs, but if you can suffer the sting long enough to see his main goal, James’ letter morphs from scorching caution into a warm hug.
Take James’ instruction on swearing oaths (5:12), which repeats Jesus’ own teaching (Matt. 5:37). So what’s wrong with swearing an oath? It uses someone (“I swear to God!”) or something (“I swear on the Bible!”) to make your word stronger. Wisdom says to tell the truth always. A wise person’s yes means yes without having to swear to God. Their “yes” or “no” is so true that an oath wouldn’t help it anyway.
When people relate to one another in that way, with that level of truth and trust, Jesus’ Kingdom expands. Whole, loving relationships mark the path walked by wise people. The foolish path leaves people broken and betrayed, unable to trust whether their neighbors are true. When people walk in wisdom, they abide in the truth, and the truth sets everyone free to trust one another.
As James closes, we see how he values right relationships as vital lifelines to spiritual health. James’ final instruction teaches mutual care for one another through good and bad times (5:13-18).
Are you sick? Ask for the community’s help and prayer. Doing well? Sing songs of praise as friends. Help one another generously. Have you sinned? Confess truthfully and ask for prayers of restoration. Wisely lean on each other for whatever life brings.
Reflection Questions
How would you summarize James’ discussion of wealth? How does his teaching on the use of resources parallel other teachings throughout the Bible?
James’ concern for the power of speech continues today. How do a person’s words reveal their priorities? How does speech honor God?

There’s no place like home. But what happens when your home starts to feel like exile? Today, we begin the first letter from Peter, which he writes to Gentile followers of Jesus in first-century Asia Minor. They were following a “new” God, which sparked suspicion and hostility from Roman neighbors at every turn. In their familiar home, these Jesus followers were starting to feel like outsiders wandering through.
So Peter offers encouragement from Hebrew Bible stories about people who had similar experiences. Old Jewish family stories for non-Jewish Christians? What does Abraham have to do with new Gentile Jesus followers in Asia Minor? Quite a lot, it turns out.
God first called Abraham to share his blessing with the entire Earth (Gen. 12:1-3). God later entrusted this calling to Abraham’s descendants, Israel, God’s “kingdom of priests” and “holy nation” (Exod. 19:3-6). Now, Peter says to his Gentile readers,
you
are that “chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation … now
you
are God’s people” (1 Pet. 2:9-10). That old story is yours, so start its next chapter well.
God always intended for his chosen, holy people to reflect his character to the world. So live like the Messiah, even (or perhaps especially) under pressure. Reject “fleshly desires that wage war against the soul” and maintain “good conduct” among all neighbors. Do this to teach others, including suspicious and hostile neighbors, about God so they know him when he returns (1 Pet. 2:11-13).
Whether you’re sojourning with Jesus in ancient Egypt, first-century Rome, or any other kingdom, sincere love for one’s neighbor remains the core characteristic in God’s family—a family of tested, gritty, and hope-filled people who trust God through every generation.
Reflection Questions
Imagine you are a Gentile Christian who reads this letter while suffering because of your faith. How might this letter encourage you while suffering? Which parts would be the most meaningful?
Take a moment to review Jeremiah’s instructions to the people of God who were living in exile in ancient Babylon (Jer. 29). What similarities do you notice between Jeremiah’s letter and today’s reading?

Yesterday, Peter reminded the Christians in Asia Minor that they now belong to a new multi-ethnic family. Today, they discover this new family identity means they join the family business, the royal priesthood.
Using the same language God used to identify the Israelites at Sinai, Peter calls all those who belong to Jesus a “kingdom of priests.” As you’ll learn in the video, priests represent Jesus and rule like him here on Earth.
Ruling like Jesus means loving like Jesus and, therefore, experiencing a life similar to his own. Jesus was willing to suffer so that he could forgive, bless generously, and bring
shalom
on Earth by resisting evil. His followers will suffer for doing the same. Jesus passed through death into resurrection life. His followers pass through baptismal “death” into new life with the Messiah. As the Messiah goes, so go his people.
That’s good news for the suffering Christians in Asia Minor because Jesus’ suffering, albeit painful, was not his end. Jesus was raised up, vindicated, and exalted, says Peter. So if you suffer and even die while serving the world as Jesus’ royal priesthood, you too will be raised up with Jesus.
To resist evil requires suffering, but suffering is never your end. God’s people, Jesus’ kingdom of priests, will resist evil in order to serve and care for every person. God promises to vindicate and exalt these people—to restore, confirm, strengthen, and support them (1 Pet. 5:10).
Reflection Questions
In 1 Peter 5:8, Peter tells his readers that their enemy is “like a roaring lion … on the prowl, looking for someone to devour.” Can you think of another biblical story that describes evil as a predator waiting to pounce? (Turn to Genesis 4:7 if you need a hint.) What do you think Peter might be teaching about resisting evil with this hyperlink?
Meditate on Peter’s story as it’s told in this letter and through the four gospels. When would you say Peter acknowledged Jesus as the true Messiah? How did Peter mature in his faith? What hope does Peter’s story offer to those trying to resist evil and walk with Jesus?

Moses on the plains of Moab, Joshua standing in Shechem, David on his deathbed, Paul shackled in prison—now, Peter facing political persecution. What do all these moments have in common? They’re all farewell scenes in Scripture.
In today’s reading, Peter, the bold disciple who walked with Jesus and spread the Gospel, says goodbye. This letter serves as a memorial of Peter’s teachings. It echoes many of the warnings and encouragements found in the Bible’s earlier farewell scenes.
Like his passionate predecessors, Peter pleads with his audience to remain faithful to God. The fisherman-turned-apostle offers his audience two paths. You can participate (or “partake”) in the “divine nature” (2 Pet. 1:4), which results in love. Or you can skip that and face divine justice on the Day of the Lord as the prophets of old warned about.
You’ll see in the video how Peter weaves moments of divine judgment and cosmic reckoning from the Hebrew Bible into his warning against corrupt leadership happening in his own day. Peter’s intense language and graphic imagery really raise eyebrows. However, his rhetoric is sincere, born out of love for his readers (and everyone). God
will
confront evil everywhere and in everyone. Those who ignore that fact do so at their own peril.
Earlier in Galilee, Jesus entrusted Peter to care for his sheep. Peter took that responsibility seriously. He’s deterring wolves and exposing terrible pitfalls around the pasture, so he’s not whispering. He’s bidding farewell by warning, teaching, and loving people with Jesus’ truth and grace (2 Pet. 3:18).
Reflection Questions
Peter’s farewell speech includes a challenge for God’s people to continue to grow in the Lord. What sorts of behaviors demonstrate goodness, knowledge, self-control, familial affection, endurance, godliness, and love?
Take a moment to review a few of the farewell speeches in the Bible: the final words of Moses (Deut. 29-33), Joshua (Josh. 23-24), David (1 Kgs. 2:1-12), and Paul (2 Tim. 4; cf. Acts 20:17-38). What key themes do you notice in these speeches and in Peter’s final letter?

Things are all good or all bad. People love, or people hate. You’re living in light, or you’re lost in darkness. Speaking in absolutes is rarely helpful, as black-and-white thinking ignores nuance and important complexities. But when a little child chases a ball toward traffic, we scream: “Stop! That will kill you!” not “Hey friend, let’s process the pros and cons of chasing that ball.”
John’s epistles are notorious for their blunt, black-and-white, all-or-nothing logic. You can almost hear him yelling, “I’m loud because I want you to live!”
John opens with an absolute: “God is light.”Very clear. Then he cranks it up, declaring that people who claim to love God but refuse to walk in God’s light, opting instead to walk in the darkness, are liars. If you think you can blend a little dark into the light, you’re deceiving yourself. This statement is bold and
loud
.
However, he also calls his readers “my little children,” a term of endearment and compassion. He assures them that he only speaks this way to prevent them from sinning. When you do sin and find yourself fearing punishment, don’t worry, John says, because Jesus will atone and advocate for you. In tomorrow’s reading (1 John 3-5), John will remind his readers that this adventure with Jesus is about being so complete, so perfected in love that we no longer fear punishment (1 John 4:18). So John is loud
and
loving.
Watch today’s video to see the beauty and deep concern behind this sermon-esque letter. For John, knowing Jesus means walking in the light and emitting God’s love while navigating the current darkness of our world.
Reflection Questions
The book of 1 John includes repetition, hyperbolic sayings, and stark contrasts. How do these rhetorical devices amplify the message of the letter?
Meditate on 1 John’s opening lines. How might they encourage recipients who face hostility from those who deny that Jesus is the Messiah? What evidence, according to 1 John, supports the historical reality of the risen Jesus?

Is it possible to capture the Bible’s essential message with three words? Probably not. But if you had to, these three from John might be the most accurate: “God is love” (1 John 4:8, 16).This phrase captures the foundation
and
culmination of the entire biblical story. Why did God create? Because God loves. Why did God rescue humanity by sending his Son? Because God loves. Why is God going to make all things new? Because God loves
and
because God is love.
How should humans respond to this God of deep, delighted love? For John, the answer is obvious: “Beloved,” he writes, “let us love” (1 John 4:7). Both “beloved” and “love” here come from the Greek word “
agape
